turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: May it please the Court. This is an appeal (inaudible) Court of Michigan. The Supreme Court of Michigan affirmed the decree of the trial court which held that the Detroit's smoke ordinance which in effect provided for the inspection and -- and approval of boilers on -- on appellant's vessels engaged in interstate commerce and which had been approved, inspected and proved and licensed by the Federal Government to navigate was applicable to -- to those vessels. And also that the penalty provision of the smoke ordinance which could not be complied with, with the boilers which had been approved, inspected and licensed by the Federal Government. The appellant claims that the Federal Government by its boiler or by its inspection laws had preempted the field and that therefore the city ordinance could not validly be applied to the vessels. It also claimed that the equipment on vessels engaged in interstate commerce required uniform treatment. And that the ordinance which also provided for inspection and approval of the same equipment and require a -- required necessary treatment and that -- and it was an undue burden upon interstate commerce. The facts are that this appellant has a mill in Alpena, Michigan and has a fleet of six vessels which -- with which it transports cement from its mill in Alpena to various ports on the Great Lakes. Five of these vessels are steam vessels and they're equipped with hand-fired Scotch marine boilers. And --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It's necessary to understand your case, to understand what -- what a marine Scotch boiler is because it's sold and you'd better explain that these -- one ignoramus to understand.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I don't think it is, Your Honor. If it is, it's merely a boiler in which you put in the fuel and the fuel heats tubes that runs over the fuel.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, this hand -- hand-fired Scotch marine has more smoke, that is emphasized.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That's right. The -- the hand-fired Scotch marine boiler is the grand dad of the boilers used on the vessels and it -- it as, Your Honor -- as Your Honor said it does admit more smoke. But these vessels are inspected annually at the time and bi-annually now by the United States Coast Guard. There, the vessels are inspected and the (inaudible) is inspected. And after this --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But it's just -- do they -- do the statutes or regulations specifically call for inspection of boilers?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes. Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Specifically.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, is that the -- the only authority for safety on stevedoring purposes or is it also for air pollution.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Well, sir, I can't point to anything in the statute that says air pollution. No. But the statute says that if the broiler is for any reason unsafe, it should not be approved. The approval says it shall be -- it shall be approved only of it's safe and in whole in part or if it's because of defective design or defective workmanship, defect of material or for any other reason is unsafe, then the boiler should be and must be (Inaudible) that purpose.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And I take that you will argue that unsafe is not restricted to safety for purposes of functioning as a boiler.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That is part of my argument. But my argument is more than that because I -- after the Federal Government has inspected these boilers, found them safe for whatever purpose the statute requires, it issues a certificate, which certificate also contains a license. And that -- that license is, the said vessel is permitted to be navigated for one year on the Great Lakes. Now, that is the license which is included in the certificate of inspection. Then there's also another certificate which the Treasury Department issues and that at the bottom of it. That is -- that and the photostat of that is contained in the transcript for the record following page 42. There at the bottom of that, there's a license. “License is hereby granted for the said vessel to be employed in carrying on the coasting and foreign trade for one year from the date hereof but no longer.” It is our -- It is my primary contention that by those two licenses, the Federal Government has specifically said that this vessel bearing in mind that the first license describes the vessel, describes the boiler and it has inspected the boilers, says that this vessel maybe used to navigate the Great Lakes for one year.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: May I ask you, Mr. Lindbloom, if Michigan might say at one of these vessels as it passed through the Detroit River should not exceed five knots per hour. Would that be in light of these licenses, these federal licenses beyond the state power?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I would doubt if it were. But there you see, there, the -- the vessel could be controlled by that speed. The point I'm getting to here is that this smoke ordinance cannot be complied with, with the equipment that has been approved and licensed.If -- if in Your Honor's question, these vessels could not go unless -- it could not go at a speed of -- of as little as five knots an hour then I would say that -- that the Detroit could -- but under the question, they could comply with the -- with the ordinance, and here, they can't.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: How old is this federal statute?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Oh, I think it goes back for many, many years probably the 1870 and through there. I'm picking -- figure out the year but it is old.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Long before the State of Morgans?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Oh, yes. Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I didn't -- I didn't quite understand the last statements you made when you said --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He doesn't argue.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And did I understand you to say that they cannot comply with the ordinance?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: They cannot.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It -- it's impossible to comply with the ordinance.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Is -- yes. That's a finding of the -- of the -- that was admitted in the trial and it's -- it's a finding in the -- of this.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean with this, “and use this boiler.”

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Use this boiler.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I thought you -- but now I understand that you're qualifying it that way.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Oh I -- that's right. It can't --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I'm not assuming that it's impossible to --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Oh no. No, no.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: We -- we could put in -- we could put in other -- we could put in another boiler or we could put in an underfeed stoker at a cost of $70,000 or $80,000 and -- or we could put in oil -- oil burning equipment at a $100,000 withheld which -- with which we could probably comply with the present Detroit's --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It's a question of cost.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: It's a question of cost to begin with. But I think there's more than that because I think there is this question of preemption.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I'm simply --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- do you think because that it's affected by preemption. As in effect, preemption.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes. The -- the -- it seems to me that Congress by -- by passing or by issuing these licenses has expressly said that this particular vessel may be used. And because -- now to go for a minute --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is there anything in the record which shows it specifically and expressly that the Government agreed that vessels which would emit this kind of smoke could be utilized despite the city ordinance?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Well, if you mean by that, I'm not quite sure I understand. If you mean by that that the Federal Government expressly say that ignoring or taking --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: -- a consideration of the Detroit ordinance?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No. I mean -- I mean, there's nothing -- there's nothing in the record.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Does it have -- does it -- does a federal Government regulation have any provisions as to smoke control at all?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Not expressly, none. None expect what I argued a moment ago that -- where they said that the boiler is unsafe for any reason.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes, that's the closest you come to any federal regulation.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Is there anything in the -- in the inspection service of the Coast Guard to -- to indicate that -- that the Government has any standards or -- or is interested in how much smoke these boilers emit?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I think to answer that candidly, I have to say there isn't anything. And this -- the Detroit smoke ordinance, first -- first, requires an inspection of any equipment that's used within the city and -- and it -- then it also provides that if a boiler emits smoke three times of the prohibited time within any 12 months period, the equipment shall be sealed and the seal shall -- shall stand unless the sea Commissioner finds that there's an improvement in the equipment.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Have they tried to do that here?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No, not yet.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: We don't -- we don't have an actual effort to do that under the ordinance. We don't yet because as I tried to explain in my reply brief, the -- there was a difference of opinion of the Attorney General in Michigan and as soon as -- soon as the city started to -- to enforce the -- the penalty provision, we got the injunction and -- but we allege in our bill of complaint that the city has threatened to enforce the ordinance against us.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Is that the only sanction with the ordinance?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Well, it can --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: To prevent the -- to prevent the -- the -- to tie the ship up? Is that the only sanction in the ordinance?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No, no. We could --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: What are the other sanctions?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That it -- its criminal, $100 fine and for -- not only the company but also the -- the officers on the boats one -- and a $100 fine, 30 days in jail or both and each day's violation is a new -- is a new penalty.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You attack the whole ordinance, the entire ordinance before they have applied any sanction to the company.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Except the criminal. They started the criminal proceedings.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: And that, they started the criminal proceedings, they issued a warrant or a ticket as they call them, against the company and against the officers of the vessel.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: And at that point, we filed the bill.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: And we allege in there that they're going to enforce the ordinance against us, and the city says in effect, they're going to force all, it's legally applicable. So I think there's a fair inference that they will enforce the -- enforce the whole thing which it requires the inspection and also a sealing if you violate the ordinance criminally three times within the 12 months period.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Do you -- has it been violated twice before and so found criminally?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No this was the -- this (Voice Overlap) first --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. Well then, in this -- in this action, they couldn't impose the final sanction of -- of tying that to your ship, could they?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: They couldn't but I mean I don't think there's any doubt that you would violate it more than three times within a year because as I say, the -- the smoke of the prohibited kind cannot be prevented with this -- with these boilers. Our problem is that we -- we dock at Detroit. It shows one of these vessels docked 26 times, we'll say the other one 22 times in a year. And they're there for about 16 hours to two -- to a full day each time. And every four hours, they have to rebuild the fire. At that time there's smoke of the prohibited kind is apt to -- to come forth. And on -- in fact, on the day in question, they were charged, each ship, with violating the smoke ordinance for -- from between nine and 10 minutes. And --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Is that when -- is that when their growing tubes as it's called or --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Building a new fire, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Is this a fire tube or water tube kind of boiler?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That I am not sure, I think it's what you call a fire tube. But I'm not wholly positive.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: May I ask you, sir? Do we have here any question of territorial jurisdictions? In other words is Lake Erie within the city limits of Detroit?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Well, the Detroit River, yes, which is a connecting river.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: And that's where you offend the claim, is that it?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes we're tied -- we -- we -- these particular offenses that we're charged with occurred when we were tied up at a dock in the Detroit River. And the Detroit -- the City of Detroit jurisdiction extends to the middle of the river. But all boats using -- engaged in the Great Lakes, going from one lake to the other must -- must use the Detroit River. And there's apparently about 250 vessels on the Great Lakes are in the same predicament. That is they're equipped with hand-fired Scotch marine boilers. And as I -- I don't think that the trial court and the Michigan Supreme Court merely held that there -- there was no preemption. Both of them paid no attention to the argument concerning the licensing. It seems to me that under Gibbons versus Ogden where a license is granted, no one else can say that this license can be used only on certain conditions.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I ask you this question. Do the -- do the examinations both by the Coast Guard and by the Bureau of Customs, they make separate examinations?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I'm not too sure that the Bureau -- that the Custom makes much of an -- the Custom issues that only if the Coast Guard has already inspected.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, does the Coast Guard inspect locally then it inspect -- it inspects --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- either in Detroit or wherever your boat --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I think they do or -- or we go somewhere where they ask us to.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But It's a -- it's a physical inspection --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Oh, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- and not a -- and not passing on -- on a written application?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No, Your Honor, they -- it's a physical examination. As matter of fact they sometime tear down the boilers. Periodically, you got to tear down the boilers, you go to operate them when they're standing there watching you.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is there a Coast Guard division of whatever it's called, is there a-- Coast Guard stationed in Detroit or in the Detroit River?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I -- yes. I think this --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did the local people examine this?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: They're -- they're local employees of the -- yes, on the -- on page 41 of the transcript or I mean the record.There, the Commander, Mr. Hansen, Commander of the United States Coast Guard is -- is stationed at Detroit, Michigan. And I think in there, it shows that the boiler, the mountings have been removed at certain times and different themes like that. Now, it's a -- it's a good faith and a very careful examination.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I'm sure to -- I assume it's in good faith.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What I want to know is --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No, I mean it's more than a -- than writ.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Because somebody who lives in Detroit, knows about Detroit ordinances, doesn't he?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I would assume so.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Does the Customs Service certificate have anything to do with safety?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I don't think so. I mean I think --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Then how is it pertinent here?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Well, it's pertinent in this respect that it is a license. That's --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: A license to do what?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: A license -- license is hereby granted for the said vessel, it's naming -- it names a vessel up above to be employed in carrying on the coasting and foreign trade for one year from the date hereon but no longer.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. But isn't that only so far as the -- the Customs laws are concerned? Isn't that all it's concerned with?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No. I think not, Your Honor, because under a statute, that license does not issue until the Coast Guard has completed their license and approved the vessel.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But if the Coast Guard didn't -- didn't certify it, it couldn't? It couldn't travel at all?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That's right. Nor would the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: So with the second. So there'd be no necessity of the Customs Service bothering about Customs on the ship if it couldn't -- if it couldn't under the Coast Guard certificate use the coastline, isn't that right?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That's right. I mean --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: -- they're both, it's -- it's just two licenses.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. But the second one means nothing in this case does it?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Because it doesn't deal -- it doesn't deal with anything but Customs.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No. I think it does more than that.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: What does it deal with? What --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: It deal -- it deals with safety in this respect that -- but it delegates the safety function to the United States Coast Guard.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, does the Customs Service have anything to do with safety?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Then why do you say it depends upon the safety?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) that the -- that the Coast Guard certifies to?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No, because under the statutes, you cannot -- the Coast -- the -- the Custom cannot issue that certificate until after the vessel has been approved --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: -- by the Coast Guard. (Voice Overlap) -- That's the only connection.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I suppose that Customs Service is -- is interested in the operation of the vessel from the standpoint of -- of the Customs.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I would assume that is right.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And -- and I would assume nothing else unless there's something in the Act to -- to indicate something else.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But if -- if it cannot -- if it doesn't have the certificate of the Coast Guard and can't operate at all, then of course there's no -- there's no reason why the -- the Customs Service should -- should grant a certificate and isn't that the only reason that they require first a certificate of the Coast Guard?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Well, that I -- I mean that maybe so, Your Honor. I mean all I know is the statute says --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: “And it shall not issue unless and until the Coast Guard issues a certificate.”

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But my point is this, if you are going to the question of preemption.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: The other one is --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And use -- and using the second -- the second certificate as an argument that the Government has preempted it. I suppose that the purpose of the certificate would have -- have something to -- to do with determining whether there was preemption so far as the emission of the smoke is concerned.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I would -- I would assume that but I think it seems to me it goes -- might go a little bit further. If the Federal Government gives a license that -- leaving out -- leaving out the -- the preemption that the Federal Government gives a license to use this vessel on navigable water can the city come along and say, "Well, you can use it only on our terms."

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, let's suppose this, that instead of this being a vessel, it was -- it's one of these big diesel trucks that goes along a -- an interstate highway and is authorized by the Interstate Commerce Commission to -- to go through the cities and it goes through the cities emitting just as much of that black smoke as it wants to emit without -- without any power in the cities to -- to restrain it?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That I don't know. As practical matter, it does [Laughs] but I mean they couldn't --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Isn't the fact of the matter, the cities do, don't they?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I don't know. I mean I -- I would think that would be a little different, Your Honor for the -- for one reason if no other and that is that this Court has granted much greater control in respect to highways than you have to -- to sea water. It seems to me that -- that our case is much closer to your -- to your Service Storage and Transfer versus Virginia which was just decided a short time ago. Or that company had a -- a certificate from the ICC and there, Virginia found that it picked up goods and delivered goods in -- in Virginia through a point in West Virginia and -- and was found that that was a fraud and it was violating the Intrastate Carrier Act and fined it for $3500. This Court said to -- to allow that fine to stand would be the same thing as a partial, a cancellation of the federal placements of the certificate.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, suppose the issue we have here in that case was not the one we did have but suppose that the trains, they're running their trains 75 miles an hour through a -- through a thickly populated district, do you think the city would be prevented from -- from regulating the speed of that to conform to the safety of the public?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No. But like (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I said --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I think, I think --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Did have, more like what we've got here?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: No, no because -- because as I said, our vessels can with the equipment as approved in the license cannot at all times comply with the ordinance. If -- you see, what I meant was, in my original bill, I asked that this -- that the city be enjoined except where the smoke was caused by our negligence or the improper use of the equipment. I'm -- I'm claiming it only where this -- only where this -- offense of smoke cannot be prohibited, cannot be prevented with -- or with the equipment which we do have.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: In short of the -- short of it -- short of your case is, isn't it? You've got a license to use a vessel with a particular equipment, if you have to supply -- if you have to obey the ordinance of Detroit, you couldn't use your vessel with the equipment. You say you got a license from Uncle Sam to use that equipment and that license is controlling. And therefore, the question is whether impliedly, there is power in the State and this is what it gets down to, to make regulation which, in and of itself is enough -- is a -- a regulation that the State may make for itself in welfare. That's the problem, isn't it?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes. It's -- it's -- the problem is whether or not -- whether or the State -- the city's regulation may -- may limit the federal license. I mean and -- and I'm also using the federal license as -- as indicating preemption of the field.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Also, we first have to determine if the federal license really license you to do.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Whether it says you may use this vessel.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: We, the United States having superior power to say, “Go ahead and use this vessel no matter what anybody says about it because we're the boss.”

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And the other suggestion is that their implications to that -- that license. May I ask you -- I do have a curiosity of what the Chief Justice said about the second license, the Customs. I take it -- am -- am I right in inferring if it is the Coast Guard, even though the calling pass must of the Coast Guard when they issued your license. The Bureau of Customs doesn't automatically have to give you its license, is that right?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That's probably. I think that's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, what help is the Customs charged with? In the first place, you can't use your vessel if you're not a -- with this Coast Guard's traffic, isn't it? Under the Coast Guard's traffic law.

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Therefore, it's only -- it's confined to American citizens. I suppose the coast -- Customs are concerned with that --

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are they not?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It has to ascertain that. Do they get any revenue? Do -- do you pay by tonnage or whatnot for getting this license?

turns.name: ALFRED E. LINDBLOOM
turns.text: I would think not, Your Honor, because we just hold for ourselves.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: We'll recess now, Mr. --
